[龍]
Pictogram ( 象形 ) – originally depicting a sacred composite creature in ancient China, combining features of the snake, crocodile, fish, deer, etc.
Shang oracle-bone and bronze forms usually show a large-mouthed beast head and an S-shaped geometric body; Shang bronzes and decorative motifs show diamond- or scale-like patterning associated with snakes (perhaps fish or crocodile scales), occasionally with legs. In oracle-bone forms, the head bears one or two inverted 王 -shaped elements (i.e. inverted axe/halberd forms) or a 玉 -like ornament. The form in Heji H04655 matches Shang bronze forms, which are more pictorial, like an inverted axe/halberd attached above the head; scholars generally derive this “bottle-shaped horn” from deer antlers, though some bronze forms instead attach a bell-shaped or shell-like undeciphered graph or clan emblem.
In Western Zhou bronzes, the head ornament tends toward 辛 ; by the late Western Zhou and early Spring and Autumn period, the mouth is drawn downward into 肉 , and the body behind the head develops a large bulge, like a snake swollen after swallowing a large animal. Thereafter head and body separate into the left and right parts of the character; by the Warring States period, the S-shaped right side also acquires three horizontal strokes like a dorsal fin. In Eastern Han stelae, the right side may, perhaps for ease of writing, change into a form like 尨 ; Sui stelae show forms written simply as 尨 . This style becomes commoner from the Song and Yuan onward; Yuan editions already show the one-stroke form 龙 , later standardized in mainland China. The Japanese form 竜 is first attested in the 597 Sui epitaph Dong Meiren Muzhi ( 《董美人墓誌》 ).

[龙]
Simplified from 𢅛 , a variant form of 龍 . 帝 is then removed, leaving out 尨 . This was simplified even further by removing two of the three strokes of 彡 .
